# Chapter One — The First Night

The brandy had been a mistake.

Arthur Penhaligon stood in the shrubbery of Ashworth Manor, feet wet from the dew-soaked grass, and calculated the distance from the trellis to the third-floor window. The trellis was old wood, choked with ivy that had died two winters ago and now clung in brittle skeleton form. The window was Eleanor's. He knew this because he had watched her light appear there each night for a week, standing in the darkness of the garden like a ghost haunting his own life.

The brandy made him feel brave and stupid in equal measure. Brave enough to reach for the first slat. Stupid enough to pull himself up.

The wood groaned.

He froze, three feet off the ground, his fingers white-knuckled on the trellis. The manor remained dark. No servants rushing with lamps, no dogs baying. He climbed another foot, and the ivy cracked under his grip, showering his sleeves with dust and desiccated leaf. His right hand found a hold that seemed stable; he shifted his weight; the hold revealed itself as rot. He slid, caught himself, felt the skin of his palm tear open on a rusted nail.

He did not swear. He could not afford to make sound.

Twenty minutes later—he had not measured time, only the progressive failure of his arms—he reached the window ledge. His breath came ragged, and sweat plastered his shirt to his back despite the autumn cold. His right hand throbbed with a deep, wet heat. He hooked his elbows over the ledge and hung there, face pressed against the cold glass, unable for a moment to remember what came next.

The room was lit by a single candle on the dressing table. He could see the edge of a bed, the corner of a wardrobe, the familiar silhouette of a woman in a dressing gown standing very still, looking at him.

Eleanor.

Her face was shadowed, but he knew the shape of her—the particular way she held her head, as though listening for something she could not quite hear. She did not move toward the window. She did not retreat. She simply stood there, watching him bleed onto her ledge.

He tapped on the glass.

She did not respond. He remembered the brandy then, the fool's courage that had carried him up the trellis, and felt it drain away. What remained was a man clinging to a window at midnight, his palm dripping, his life reduced to this single, ridiculous gesture.

He tapped again. "Eleanor."

His voice came out hoarse. He cleared his throat. "Eleanor, please."

She moved then—two steps to the window, her hand rising to the latch. She stopped with her fingers on the iron. He could see her face now, the same face he had known since childhood, the same dark eyes and uncertain mouth. Older. Two years older. Tired in a way he did not remember.

"You shouldn't be here." Her voice was muffled by the glass but clear enough.

"I know."

"Go away, Arthur."

"I tried." He laughed, and it came out wrong, jagged. "I tried the front door three times. Your housekeeper remembers me from before. She told me your family is not at home to visitors. She told me this with her face quite straight, as though I hadn't skated on your pond when we were twelve."

She did not smile. Her fingers tightened on the latch but did not lift it.

"They'll call for help," she said. "If you wake the house, they'll call the constable. My father—"

"I won't wake the house."

"You're bleeding."

He looked down at his hand, the dark smear on the white paint of the window frame. "It's nothing."

"It's not nothing. You're bleeding on my mother's roses."

He almost laughed again. The absurdity of it—this was what she chose to say. Not how did you get here, not why have you come, but a rebuke about the rosebed.

"Let me in, Ellie. Five minutes. Then I'll go."

Her jaw tightened. She hated being called Ellie. He had forgotten this, or perhaps he remembered it and used it deliberately, a small cruelty to puncture the formal distance between them.

"Five minutes," she repeated.

She lifted the latch. The window swung inward, and he tumbled through in a graceless collapse of limbs, catching himself on the edge of her dressing table. The candle flickered. He stood in Eleanor Blackwood's bedroom, breathing hard, his hand dripping onto the carpet.

She closed the window behind him. The click of the latch sounded very loud.

They regarded each other in the candlelight. She was thinner than he remembered, the bones of her wrists sharp where her sleeves fell back. Her hair was plaited for sleep, a dark rope over one shoulder. She wore a dressing gown of pale blue that he did not recognize—new, he thought, part of the life she lived now, the life of Miss Blackwood of Ashworth Manor.

"You're dripping," she said.

He looked down. A drop of blood had fallen on the carpet near his boot, a dark spot spreading into the fibers.

"Sorry." He fumbled for his handkerchief, wrapped it around his palm. The white linen darkened immediately. "I'm sorry. I shouldn't have come. I'll go."

"No." She crossed to him, took his wrist, examined the wound with a clinical detachment that reminded him of his sister examining a child's scraped knee. "It's deep. You'll need it cleaned."

"I'll clean it at home."

"You'll clean it here." She released his wrist and moved to the washstand, pouring water into a basin. "Sit."

He sat on the edge of her bed because there was nowhere else. The room was smaller than he remembered from childhood—no, not smaller, but fuller, crowded with the furniture of a woman's life. A dressing table crowded with bottles and brushes. A writing desk buried under correspondence. Books stacked on the floor beside the wardrobe, more books than any young lady was supposed to read.

She brought the basin and a clean cloth, knelt before him, and took his hand without asking permission. The water was warm. Her fingers were efficient.

"I heard about the engagement," he said.

Her hands paused, then resumed their work. "I imagined you might have. The village is not large."

"Creswell."

"Yes."

"I didn't know him before. The family, I mean. New money."

"Arthur." She looked up at him. "Don't."

"Don't what?"

"Don't do this. Don't come here and say his name like that. Don't pretend you care about his money when what you mean is something else entirely."

"What do I mean?"

She looked back down at his hand. The cloth had pinked; his blood diluted in the warm water. "You mean that he has what you don't. You mean that doors open for him. You mean that he can walk through the front entrance of this house and I will be brought down to meet him in the drawing room, and tea will be served, and everything will be proper."

The word proper came out edged.

"Is that what you want?" he asked. "Proper?"

"What I want hasn't mattered for two years." She wrapped the cloth around his palm, tied it with a strip of linen from her pocket. "There. Hold it elevated."

He didn't move. "Eleanor."

"Don't say my name like that."

"Like what?"

"Like you're sorry for me." She stood, took the basin to the washstand, emptied it through the window into the night. "I'm not a tragedy, Arthur. I'm a woman making the best of circumstances. My father inherited a title and eleven thousand pounds of debt. My mother's health—she has attacks, nerves, the doctors don't agree on what to call it. My sister is fourteen and believes she will be presented in London and marry a duke." Eleanor turned back to him, her face half in shadow. "Mr. Creswell has offered to settle the debts. All of them. In exchange for my hand."

"A transaction."

"Most marriages are. We simply admit ours."

He stood. The room felt smaller with both of them standing, the bed between them. "I wouldn't call it a transaction."

"You couldn't afford to make the offer." Her voice was not cruel, only tired. "That's not a judgment. It's arithmetic."

"I came to tell you—" He stopped. What had he come to tell her? That he loved her? That he had loved her since they were sixteen and she lent him a book of poems and never asked for it back? That he had watched her carriage leave the village two years ago and felt like a limb was being severed?

"Tell me what?" she asked.

"That I'm building a bridge in Cornwall. That the firm has promoted me. That I—" He ran his uninjured hand through his hair. "I don't know what I came to tell you."

She studied him. The candle was burning low; the room grew dimmer by degrees.

"You came to climb a trellis," she said finally. "You came to bleed on my window ledge. You came because you couldn't not come. And now you've come, and you see that nothing has changed."

"Everything has changed."

"Nothing that matters." She moved past him to the window, looked out at the dark garden. "You should go. Before the servants wake. Before my mother's maid comes to draw my curtains."

He crossed to the window, stood beside her. Below, the garden was a black expanse, the trellis a shadow against the stone.

"I'll come back," he said.

"No."

"I'll come back tomorrow night."

"Arthur—"

"Tell me not to come, and I won't. Mean it, and I'll believe you. But don't tell me not to come because you're afraid. Don't tell me not to come because proper young ladies don't have men climbing through their windows. Tell me you don't want to see me, and I'll walk out that door and never return."

She didn't speak. The silence stretched, filled with the small sounds of night—a distant owl, the settling of the old house, the candle's flame consuming itself.

"Your hand will need fresh linen tomorrow," she said at last. "If you climb again, you'll tear the wound open."

"Is that a yes?"

"It's not a no." She turned from the window, and for a moment, in the dying candlelight, he saw the girl he remembered—the one who caught minnows with her skirts kilted up, who read novels behind the hymnal in church, who looked at him once when they were seventeen and said, "When I marry, I want it to be for love, and I want everyone to disapprove."

He had laughed. He had said, "Then you'll have to marry me."

She hadn't laughed. She had looked at him with an expression he hadn't understood then and wasn't sure he understood now.

"The trellis won't hold," she said. "There's a drainage pipe on the east side. It looks rusted, but I've seen the servants climb it to clear leaves from the gutter."

He laughed, and this time it came out right—not bitter, not desperate, but wondering. "You've thought about this."

"I've thought about a great many things, Arthur Penhaligon. Now go. Before I change my mind."

He climbed out the window. The drainage pipe, when he found it, held his weight. By the time he reached the garden, her light had been extinguished.

He walked home through the dark, his hand throbbing, his coat ruined, his heart beating in a way that had nothing to do with exertion.
step_outputs.7:
# Chapter Two — The Arrangement

The morning after Arthur Penhaligon climbed through her window, Eleanor woke with the certainty of exposure.

She lay in her bed, watching the light change behind her curtains, waiting for footsteps on the stairs, for her mother's voice raised in accusation, for the housemaid's knowing look over the breakfast tray. The wound on Arthur's hand had left a stain on her carpet—small, dark, easily explained if anyone noticed. No one would notice. The servants saw nothing they were not paid to see.

She rose, dressed, went downstairs.

Her mother sat in the morning room, a piece of embroidery stretched on its frame, her face composed in its usual mask of mild discontent. Mrs. Blackwood had been a beauty in her youth; now, at fifty, she preserved the remnants through careful application of rice powder and a reluctance to go outdoors. Her "nerves" kept her from society, but not from its machinations.

"You're pale," she said, without looking up from her stitching. "I hope you're not becoming ill. The engagement dinner is tomorrow."

"I'm not ill, Mama."

"Sit. You're making me anxious, standing there."

Eleanor sat. The morning room was cold despite the fire; her father had ordered the coal rationed, a directive that had not been explained to her but whose meaning she understood. Poverty required such small surrenders—colder rooms, fewer candles, staff who left and were not replaced.

"Mr. Creswell," her mother said, cutting a thread with small silver scissors, "will expect a certain standard. I've spoken to Cook about the menu. It will be adequate, though not what we would have offered three years ago." She sighed, a sound Eleanor had learned to read as self-pity disguised as martyrdom. "Your father has insisted on the good claret. I told him it was extravagant, but he says we must make an impression. He says Creswell will expect us to live as—as people of our position ought."

Our position. Eleanor's father had been a country solicitor until a distant cousin died without issue, leaving him a title, a manor house in advanced decay, and debts accumulated by three generations of careless men. They had been poor before, but it was a manageable poverty—the poverty of people who expected nothing different. Now they were poor in a way that required pretense, and the pretense was expensive.

"Mama." Eleanor heard her own voice as though from a distance. "How much do we owe?"

The needle paused mid-stitch. "That is not a matter for—"

"How much?"

Her mother set down the embroidery. Her face had gone very still, the particular stillness that preceded either tears or anger. "You know we have debts. Your father's inheritance came with—"

"I know there are debts. I want to know what they are. I want to understand what I'm—what this marriage is buying."

The word hung between them, ugly in its accuracy. Her mother's lips thinned.

"Eleven thousand pounds," she said finally. "In secured notes. There are other obligations—tradesmen, the estate's maintenance, your father's attempts to—" She stopped. "The creditors have been patient because of the title. They expect us to make a suitable match. They expect the family to be... restored."

"And if I don't marry Mr. Creswell?"

"Then your sister will have to marry someone. Flora is fourteen. In four years she will be presented, and what dowry will we have? What connections? What prospects?" Her mother leaned forward. "This is not about you, Eleanor. I know that is difficult to accept. But you are the eldest. You have the opportunity to save this family. Flora's future depends on your decision."

Flora, who was still a child, who dreamed of London balls and handsome dukes, who had no idea that her sister was being traded for her chances.

"And if I refuse?"

Her mother picked up her embroidery again. "You won't. You're a good daughter. You understand duty."

---

Mr. Creswell arrived at four o'clock.

Eleanor watched from the upstairs window as his carriage rolled up the drive—a smart, well-sprung vehicle that spoke of money without pretension. He stepped out carefully, adjusting his coat, looking up at the manor with an expression she could not read. Not awe. Something more tentative, a man arriving at a house he was not sure would welcome him.

She had met him three times. The first at the assembly rooms in Bath, where her mother had positioned her near the refreshments and Mr. Creswell had fetched her lemonade without being asked. The second at a dinner party given by neighbors, where he had sat beside her and spoken hesitantly of his family's mills, of the workers they employed, of his hope to do some good with the wealth he had inherited. The third was when he had asked her father for permission to pay his addresses—a scene Eleanor had not witnessed but whose outline she knew: her father's exclamation of pleasure, her mother's satisfied smile, the understanding that Mr. Creswell's settlement would clear the debts and provide a comfortable allowance for the rest of their lives.

She had not been consulted. She had agreed, of course. What else could she do?

Now she came downstairs, wearing the blue dress her mother had selected, her hair arranged by the maid, her face composed into the expression she had learned to wear: pleasant, receptive, giving nothing away.

Mr. Creswell was in the drawing room, standing awkwardly near the fireplace as though uncertain whether to sit. He was not a handsome man—his face was too soft for that, his eyes too small, his forehead too high. But there was kindness in his expression, an eagerness to please that might have been endearing in other circumstances.

"Miss Blackwood." He bowed. "You look—that is, the color becomes you. Blue. It's a fine color."

"Thank you, Mr. Creswell. Will you sit?"

He sat. She sat. The silence stretched between them, filled by the ticking of the clock and the muffled sounds of Cook preparing dinner.

"I brought you something," he said suddenly, and produced from his coat pocket a small box. "It's not—I hope it's not too forward. My sister helped me choose it. I don't know much about—about what ladies prefer."

Eleanor opened the box. Inside was a brooch: a small silver bird, its wings spread, a single garnet for an eye. It was not ostentatious. It was, she realized with a sickening lurch, exactly what she might have chosen for herself.

"It's beautiful," she said. "Thank you."

"You don't have to wear it. If you don't like it."

"I like it." She looked up at him, and what she saw in his face undid her: he was nervous. He was terrified. This man with eleven thousand pounds at his disposal was sitting in her shabby drawing room, offering her a silver bird, hoping she would not find him wanting.

"I look forward to—" He cleared his throat. "That is, I hope we might—I know this is not a romantic attachment. I understand that. But I want you to know that I will endeavor to be a good husband. I will try, Miss Blackwood. I will try every day to make you glad you chose me."

Chose. As though she had a choice.

"I'm sure you will be," she said, and smiled, and watched him relax into the belief that everything was well.

---

That night, she waited for Arthur.

She told herself she would not let him in. She had resolved it during dinner, watching Mr. Creswell smile at her across the table, watching her mother's face soften with satisfaction, watching her father drink too much of the good claret and become maudlin about the estate's former glories. She would tell Arthur that it was done. That she was promised. That he must not come again.

She lit her candle at ten. At eleven, she moved her chair to face the window. At midnight, she was still sitting there, her hands twisted in her lap, the brooch Mr. Creswell had given her lying on the dressing table like an accusation.

He appeared at half past twelve.

His climb was faster tonight; she had watched him find the drainage pipe, scale it with the practiced movements of a man who had studied the route. When he reached her window, he was not breathing as hard as before, but his coat was damp with rain she hadn't noticed falling.

She opened the window before he could knock.

"You came," she said.

"I said I would."

"It's raining."

"I know." He climbed through, more graceful than the first night, and stood in her room dripping onto the carpet. His hand was wrapped in fresh linen, but she could see the stain seeping through. "How was your day?"

She laughed—a sharp, involuntary sound. "My day. I was fitted for a wedding dress. I accepted a brooch from my fiancé. I watched my mother count the silver and decide which pieces could be sold without the neighbors noticing." She turned away from him. "My day was fine, Arthur. How was yours?"

He didn't answer. When she turned back, he was standing very close, close enough that she could smell the rain on him, the wool of his coat, something underneath that was simply Arthur.

"Tell me," he said. "Tell me everything."

So she did.

She told him about the eleven thousand pounds. About the creditors who called at the house when they thought no one was watching. About her mother's fragile health, which might not survive a scandal. About Flora, fourteen years old, whose future was being written in Eleanor's blood.

He listened without interrupting. When she finished, he was silent for a long moment.

"There must be another way," he said finally.

"There isn't."

"I could borrow. The firm—I have connections. There are men who would lend—"

"At what interest? And what would you offer as security? Your good name?" She shook her head. "You're an architect, Arthur. A good one, perhaps, but you're twenty-seven years old and you rent two rooms above a bookshop. You have nothing to offer but yourself, and as touching as that is—" She heard the cruelty in her voice and didn't stop it. "As touching as that is, it won't pay the butcher."

He recoiled as though she had struck him. "I didn't come here to be shamed."

"Why did you come?"

"Because I couldn't stay away." The words came out raw, unguarded. "Because every night since I heard about your engagement, I've lain awake thinking of you in this house, with those people, being sold to the highest bidder. Because I remember the girl you were, and I cannot—"

"That girl is dead." Eleanor's voice cracked. "She died when her father inherited a title and eleven thousand pounds of debt. She died when she understood that her life was not her own, that it never had been, that she was a commodity to be traded for her family's survival. That girl you remember—she never existed. She was a child who didn't know what the world cost."

"Ellie—"

"Don't call me that." She turned away, blinking hard. "The girl you called Ellie could afford to dream. She could afford to catch minnows and read novels and believe in love. I can't. I don't have that luxury."

Silence. The rain had stopped; she could hear water dripping from the eaves.

"What do you want?" Arthur asked quietly. "If you could have anything—if money and duty and your family didn't exist—what would you want?"

She wanted to say him. She wanted to say freedom. She wanted to say a life where she could want things without counting their cost.

"I don't know," she said. "I've forgotten how to want."

He moved closer. She felt him there, just behind her, not touching.

"I haven't," he said. "I want you. I've wanted you since we were sixteen and you lent me that book of poems. I wanted you when you left, and I want you now, and I will want you when you're married to someone else and living in a house I'll never enter. That's not a plan. It's not a solution. It's just what's true."

She turned. His face was close, wet with rain, his eyes bright with something that might have been tears.

"I didn't ask you to save me," she said. "I only wanted to see you."

"I know."

"I don't know what to do."

"Neither do I."

They stood there, in the candlelight, not touching. Outside, the clouds parted; moonlight fell across the floor. Somewhere in the house, a clock chimed three.

"Stay," she said. "Don't go yet."

He stayed. They sat side by side on the edge of her bed, not touching, not speaking, watching the candle burn down to nothing. When dawn began to gray the window, he climbed back through without a word.

She watched him descend into the garden, a dark shape against the lightening sky, and thought: this is what wanting feels like. This is the shape of it, the weight of it, the particular ache of having something you cannot keep.
step_outputs.8:
# Chapter Three — The Cost

The storm came from the west, over the moors, and caught Arthur halfway up the drainage pipe.

He had known it was coming—had watched the clouds bank and blacken through his office window, heard the clerks murmur about flooding in the lower streets—but he had set out anyway. Three days since he'd last seen her. Three days of drafting bridge plans he couldn't remember, of his sister's concerned questions, of sleep that came in fragments and left him more tired than before. Three days was too long. He had discovered, in the long hours between, that he had become a man who could not endure three days.

The wind hit him when he was twenty feet up, and the pipe shuddered.

He pressed himself against the stone, cheek to the cold wet wall, and waited. The rain came next—not a drizzle but a deluge, as though the sky had opened to pour out all its accumulated grief. Within seconds he was soaked through, his coat heavy, his fingers numb. The pipe, which had held his weight easily in dry weather, now felt treacherous, the iron slick beneath his grip.

He should go down. He should go home, dry himself, try again tomorrow.

He climbed higher.

Her window was a yellow square in the dark, rain-blurred and distant. He watched it as he climbed, the way a drowning man watches a shore. When he reached the ledge, his foot slipped; he caught himself with his right hand, and the wound, not yet healed, split open. The pain was immediate and blinding. He gasped, clung, hauled himself over the ledge with his left arm, and collapsed onto the stones.

The window opened. Eleanor's face appeared, pale and frightened.

"Arthur—"

He tried to answer, but his teeth were chattering too hard to form words. She reached out, grabbed his coat, pulled. He tumbled through the window in a graceless heap, landing on the carpet in a pool of rainwater and— he realized dimly— his own blood.

"Oh God." She was kneeling beside him, her hands hovering, not knowing where to touch. "Your hand—Arthur, your hand—"

"It's nothing." The words came out slurred. Cold, he thought. I'm very cold.

"You're shaking."

"I'll stop."

"You're bleeding on my carpet again."

He laughed, and it turned into a cough. She stood, disappeared, returned with blankets—her own blankets, he realized, from her bed. She wrapped them around his shoulders, pulled the wet coat from his arms, and then she was kneeling again, taking his ruined hand in both of hers.

The wound had opened completely. He could see it now, in the candlelight—a gash across his palm, raw and weeping, the edges white where the skin had torn. It should have hurt more. He suspected it would hurt more, later, when the cold stopped protecting him.

"Sit," she commanded.

He sat. She brought the basin, the cloth, the strips of linen she must have prepared in advance, and then she knelt before him and began to clean the wound.

Her hands were gentle. This was what he noticed first—the care in her touch, the way she held his wrist steady while she worked. Her fingers were warm where his skin was cold, and the warmth spread up his arm, into his chest, settling somewhere behind his ribs.

"Look at me," she said. "You're shivering too hard. I can't work if you're—"

He looked at her. Her face was close, bent over his hand, her brow furrowed in concentration. A strand of hair had escaped her plait and fell across her cheek. She tucked it back with an absent motion, never stopping her work.

"Why do you do this?" she asked.

"Do what?"

"Climb. In a storm. Tear yourself open." She pressed the cloth to his palm, and he hissed. "For what? For a few hours in this room? For conversations that go nowhere?"

"Because I have to."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one I have."

She looked up at him then, her eyes dark, her expression unreadable. The cloth in her hand had gone red.

"You could fall," she said. "The pipe could give. You could slip on the stones and—and I would hear it. I would hear you fall, and I couldn't do anything, and I would have to explain—"

"I won't fall."

"You don't know that."

"I won't fall." He said it with a certainty he didn't feel. "I've calculated the loads. The pipe will hold two hundred pounds if the fixings are sound. I've checked them."

She stared at him. "You've checked—when? When did you have time to—"

"During the day. Before the first night. I walked around the house, tested the stonework, looked for weaknesses." He tried to smile. "I'm an architect, Ellie. I know how buildings fail."

Her expression softened, or perhaps only shifted. She returned to his hand, winding the linen around his palm in neat, overlapping loops.

"You're mad," she said quietly.

"I know."

"The things you do—this—" She gestured at the window, the storm, his shivering form. "It isn't reasonable. It isn't sensible."

"No."

"Any sensible person would give up. Any sensible person would accept that this—that us—is impossible."

"Yes."

She tied off the bandage and sat back on her heels. His hand was wrapped in clean white linen, the bleeding stopped, the pain settling into a dull throb.

"I'm not sensible either," she said. "I should send you away. I should never have let you in. I should be grateful for Mr. Creswell and my family's salvation and a life that doesn't require anyone to climb drainage pipes in the rain."

"Why don't you?"

"Because—" She stopped. Her hands were resting on her thighs, and he saw that they were trembling. "Because when you're not here, I count the hours. Because I watch the window the way I used to watch the road when my father was late returning from court. Because I've started to dread the morning, and the only time I don't dread is—" She stood abruptly, turning away. "It doesn't matter."

"It matters to me."

She didn't answer. The storm had lessened outside; the rain came softer now, tapping against the glass. The candle on her dressing table flickered in some imperceptible draft.

"Tell me something," he said. "Tell me anything. I want to hear your voice."

"What would you have me say?"

"Tell me about before. Before the inheritance. Before any of this."

She was silent for so long he thought she wouldn't answer. Then she moved to the window, stood looking out at the dark, and spoke.

"I used to catch fish in the stream behind our house. Did you know that? Not trout, nothing so grand. Minnows. They were tiny, barely longer than my finger, and they gathered in the shallows where the water ran slow." Her voice had gone distant, soft. "I would wade in with my skirts kilted up, and the mud would squelch between my toes, and my mother would call from the house that I was ruining my shoes."

"I remember."

She turned. "You remember?"

"I was there. Twice, maybe three times. You taught me how to cup my hands so they couldn't slip through."

"You were terrible at it."

"I was terrible at it," he agreed. "You caught a dozen to my one. And then you put them all back."

"Of course I put them back. They weren't for keeping. They were just—for that moment, when I held something alive in my hands, something that had been wild and was now, briefly, mine." She came back to him, sat on the edge of the bed beside his chair. "I miss that. I miss the person who believed that catching minnows was enough."

"She wasn't so different from you."

"She was free."

"You're not free now because you choose not to be."

She looked at him sharply. "That's not fair."

"No. It isn't." He reached for her hand, stopped himself. "I'm sorry. I have no right to judge you. I don't know what I would do in your place. I only know that you are still the same person—the one who caught minnows, who read novels behind hymnals, who looked at me once when we were seventeen and said—"

"What?"

He shook his head. "It doesn't matter."

"Tell me."

The rain had stopped. The room was very quiet.

"You said, 'When I marry, I want it to be for love, and I want everyone to disapprove.'"

She laughed—a small sound, startled out of her. "I said that?"

"You did."

"I was a child."

"You were right."

She looked at him, and the distance between them seemed to shrink. He could see the pulse in her throat, the slight rise and fall of her breathing.

"I watched for you," she said suddenly. "After we moved. I watched the road when we drove into the village. I looked for you in the shops, at the post office, anywhere I might—" She stopped. "I thought you would come. I told myself it didn't matter if you didn't, but I watched anyway."

"I didn't come because I thought you'd changed. I thought you'd become one of them—people with titles, people who lived in houses like this. I thought you'd forgotten me."

"I tried to forget you." Her voice was barely above a whisper. "It was easier than wanting."

They sat in silence. The storm had passed entirely; through the window, he could see stars emerging between the clouds. His hand throbbed, his clothes were still damp, and he was shivering again despite the blankets.

"Arthur."

"Yes."

"I'm glad you came."

"I'm glad too."

She leaned her head against his shoulder. The gesture was small, almost accidental, but he felt it through his whole body. He didn't move. He didn't breathe. Her hair smelled of lavender soap.

"I don't know what happens now," she said.

"Neither do I."

"I'm still going to marry him. I don't see any way—I can't see a way out."

"I know."

"But I want you to keep coming. Is that selfish? It's selfish. I'm asking you to climb a drainage pipe in storms so I can have a few hours of—of something I can't keep."

He reached for her hand, and this time he didn't stop himself. His bandaged palm rested in her lap, her fingers closing over his.

"It isn't keeping," he said. "It's having. Even temporary things count."

"Do they?"

"They have to. Otherwise nothing counts."

She was quiet for a long time. The candle burned low, sputtered, began to gutter. Neither of them moved to replace it.

"My father's garden," she said eventually, her voice sleepy. "Do you remember? Before the inheritance. The roses he grew. He spent hours out there, pruning, training the climbers. He was happy then. We both were."

"I remember."

"He doesn't garden anymore. He says the grounds are too extensive, he can't manage them alone. But really I think he just—he lost something when we moved here. He lost the part of himself that believed effort was enough."

"Effort isn't always enough."

"No." She shifted against him, settling. "But sometimes it's all you have."

They sat together in the darkness, her head on his shoulder, his hand in her lap, and watched the last of the candle die. When dawn began to lighten the window, she stirred, lifted her head.

"You should go."

"I know."

"Tomorrow?"

"Tomorrow."

He climbed down into a world washed clean by rain, the stones wet beneath his hands, the drainage pipe cold but solid. His bandaged hand held. The stars were fading in the east.

He walked home through the empty streets, his heart full of something he couldn't name, something that felt like grief and joy tangled together so tightly he couldn't tell them apart.

# Chapter Four — The Empty Night

The first night, she told herself it was nothing.

She had lit the candle at ten, as was her habit now—a habit of less than a week, and already so entrenched she could not imagine breaking it. She had arranged her chair to face the window, smoothed her skirts, prepared the basin of water and the clean linen in case his hand had opened again. Then she had waited.

At eleven, she told herself he was late. The hour was not unreasonable; he had come at midnight before, at one, at half-past. He had his own life, his work, obligations she did not know about. Perhaps a client had kept him. Perhaps the weather, which had been fair all day, had turned somewhere beyond her view.

At midnight, she moved to the window and pressed her hand against the glass. It was cold. The garden below was a dark expanse, the drainage pipe a shadow against the stone. She could see the trellis, the dead ivy, the route he had taken that first night before she had told him about the pipe. The moon was half-full; it cast everything in silver and black.

At one, she began to imagine disasters.

He had fallen. The pipe had given way, the fixings loosened by rain and time, and he had slipped in the dark. He was lying even now at the base of the wall, broken, calling for help that would not come. Or he had been seen—some servant, some neighbor, someone who had reported a man climbing the manor walls, and now he was in a cell, charged with attempted—who knew what they would call it. Burglary. Trespass. Something worse.

Or, and this thought came later, sliding into her mind like a knife: he had simply not come. He had thought better of it. He had awakened to the impossibility of their situation, the futility of climbing night after night to a woman who could offer him nothing but her presence in a dark room. He had remembered, as she could not allow herself to remember, that she was promised to another man, that she would marry Mr. Creswell, that whatever this was could not lead anywhere.

At two, she moved from the window to her bed. She lay down without undressing, stared at the ceiling, listened to the sounds of the house. Her mother's cough in the room below. A servant's footsteps, padding somewhere distant. The settling of old timber.

At three, she slept.

She woke to light—a gray, watery dawn seeping through the curtains. The candle had burned to a stub; wax pooled on the dressing table. She rose, went to the window, looked down.

The garden was empty. The pipe showed no sign of disturbance.

---

"Your color is poor," her mother said over breakfast.

Eleanor looked up from her plate. She had been staring at her eggs, watching them congeal, unable to eat.

"I slept badly."

"That's the third time this week you've said so. Perhaps you should ask Mrs. Garrett for a draft. Laudanum, something to help you rest."

"I don't want laudanum."

Her mother frowned. The morning light was unkind to her; she looked older than her years, her skin papery, her eyes shadowed. The "nerves" had been worse lately, Eleanor knew—her mother spoke of attacks more frequently, retired to her room more often. Whether the condition was real or convenient, Eleanor had never been able to determine.

"The wedding invitations go out next week," her mother continued. "Your father has finalized the contract with Mr. Creswell's solicitors. The settlement is—generous. More than generous." She paused, as though waiting for Eleanor to express gratitude.

"I'm sure it is."

"Flora will be presented next season. In London. Mr. Creswell has connections—he's offered to sponsor her, introduce her to the right families." Her mother's voice softened. "You're saving us, Eleanor. You understand that. Whatever sacrifices you feel you're making, they're not in vain."

The word *sacrifice* hung in the air. She had not said it. Her mother had supplied it for her.

"May I be excused? I have a headache."

Her mother waved a dismissive hand, and Eleanor rose and left the room before her expression could betray her.

---

The second night, she determined not to wait.

She extinguished her candle at nine and got into bed. She lay in darkness, eyes closed, breathing deliberately. This was what normal people did. They slept. They did not sit by windows, watching for shapes against the stars. They did not arrange basins and linen for wounds they had not yet seen.

At ten, she opened her eyes. The room was black; clouds obscured the moon. She stared into the darkness and thought about Arthur—his hand in her lap, his head beside hers on the pillow that last night, the way he had said *I want you* as though it were the simplest thing in the world.

At eleven, she got up.

She went to the window in the dark, feeling her way, and pressed her forehead against the glass. It was colder than the night before. A frost was settling; she could see it whitening the lawn below, silvering the bare branches of the oaks.

He wasn't coming. Something had happened—something bad or something ordinary, and she would never know which. He might be dead. He might be bored. He might have simply remembered that she was not worth climbing for.

The thought caught her off guard, and she found herself crying—not the delicate tears of a heroine in a novel but ugly, gasping sobs that she pressed into her sleeve to muffle. She cried for him, for herself, for the minnows she had caught as a child and put back because she couldn't bear to keep anything that didn't want to be kept.

When the tears subsided, she was shivering. The cold had seeped through the glass, through her nightdress, into her bones. She climbed back into bed and pulled the covers to her chin.

She slept, eventually. And dreamed of nothing.

---

The third day, she saw Mr. Creswell.

He came to call in the afternoon, proper and expected, bearing a nosegay of hothouse flowers that must have cost more than her father spent on coal in a month. She received him in the drawing room, with her mother present as chaperone, and smiled in what she hoped was an appropriate manner.

"You look tired," he said, with genuine concern. "I hope you're not unwell."

"Just the heat," she lied. The room was cold; her mother had forbidden a fire until evening.

"The heat, yes. It can be oppressive, even in autumn." He smiled, eager to agree, eager to please. "I've been thinking about the house, actually—our house, in Lancashire. It's not so large as this, of course, but it's comfortable. The air is good. I think you'll find it suits you."

She had not thought about where she would live after the wedding. It seemed absurd now—not to have considered it. She would leave this place, this room, this window, and go to a house she had never seen, in a county where she knew no one.

"I'm sure I shall," she said.

"My sister has offered to help with the preparations. She has excellent taste—better than mine, I freely admit. She's suggested we might redecorate the morning room. Yellow, she thinks. A warm yellow, to catch the light."

"Yellow is lovely."

"You don't sound certain."

She looked at him—really looked, for the first time in days. His face was earnest, his expression anxious. He wanted her to be happy. He was trying, in his limited way, to build a life that would please her. And she was sitting here, nodding at his suggestions, while her heart lay in pieces in a cold bedroom upstairs.

"I'm sure yellow will be perfect," she said. "Thank you for thinking of it."

He beamed. The conversation moved on—arrangements, timelines, the details of a wedding that felt increasingly like a funeral.

When he left, she watched from the window as his carriage rolled away. The frost had melted; the garden was brown and bare. Somewhere beyond the trees, beyond the grounds she could not see, was a road that led to a village where a man named Arthur Penhaligon lived in two rooms above a bookshop.

She did not know if he was alive or dead. She did not know if he was hurt or whole. She only knew that he had not come, and she had not known how much she had depended on his coming until he stopped.

---

The third night, she determined to end it.

She lit her candle at the usual hour—ten—because some habits could not be broken in a day. She sat in her chair, faced the window, and made herself a promise: if he did not come tonight, she would not wait again. She would blow out the candle and leave it unlit. She would become who she had been before—Miss Blackwood, dutiful daughter, future Mrs. Creswell. She would stop wanting things she could not have.

She watched the window.

At eleven, she watched.

At midnight, she watched.

At one, at two, at the small cold hours that felt like drowning, she watched.

At half past two, a sound—a scraping, a shifting. She leaned forward, her heart seizing, and saw a shape against the stars. Movement. Someone on the pipe.

She rose, went to the window, looked down. The shape was there—climbing, ascending, coming toward her.

Her hand went to the latch.

Then she stopped.

The shape moved again, and she saw it clearly: a branch, broken from the oak by wind, caught on the pipe and shifting in the breeze. Nothing more. No one. Just wood and wind and the emptiness she had been trying not to feel.

She stood at the window for a long time. The candle burned behind her; the cold came through the glass; the branch shifted and settled, shifted and settled.

Eventually, she went back to her chair. She sat, and she watched, and she did not sleep. When dawn came, gray and reluctant, she rose and went to the window one last time.

The garden was empty. The pipe was clear. The world went on, indifferent to her waiting.

She returned to her chair and sat in silence as her candle finally guttered and died.
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter Five — The Return

The fever had broken at last.

Arthur stood in his sister's kitchen, watching Catherine wipe her youngest's face with a cool cloth. The child—Thomas, three years old and too small for his age—slept finally, his breathing rattling but steady. For four days the fever had raged, and for four days Arthur had barely left this house, sleeping in a chair by the bed, eating whatever Catherine pressed into his hands.

"He'll be fine now." Catherine's voice was hoarse; she hadn't slept either. "The doctor says the worst is past."

Arthur nodded. His body felt hollowed out, emptied by worry and exhaustion. He couldn't remember when he'd last changed his shirt; his hand, the one he'd sliced open on Eleanor's trellis, throbbed with a dull infection he'd been ignoring.

"You should go home," Catherine said, reading his face. "Sleep in your own bed. You're no use to anyone like this."

"I'll go."

"Arthur." She caught his arm as he turned. "There's something else. Something you've been—not saying. Where do you go at night? Don't tell me the office; I've seen you leave at midnight and come back at dawn."

He looked at his sister—her tired eyes, her stained dress, the baby whimpering in the next room. She had married a good man, a clerk in his firm, and they were happy, after their fashion. They would never be wealthy, but they had each other, and their children, and a small house with a garden where Thomas could recover from fevers.

"I've been seeing someone," he said.

Catherine's face softened. "Someone? Who? Why haven't you—"

"It's complicated."

"How complicated?"

He thought about Eleanor, waiting in her window. He thought about the three nights he'd been absent, the messages he hadn't been able to send. He thought about what it meant to need someone so desperately that three days felt like a death.

"Complicated enough that I need to go to her now. Tonight. Before I lose my mind."

Catherine studied him. "You look terrible."

"I know."

"She must be something, to put that look on your face."

"She is."

He left before she could ask more.

---

The climb was easy.

He had worried, during the long coach ride from Catherine's village, that the days away would have undone something—that his body would have forgotten the motions, that the drainage pipe would have rusted through in his absence, that some essential magic would have dissipated. But his hands remembered what his mind had been too tired to recall, and he ascended quickly, efficiently, as though he had been climbing to Eleanor's window his whole life.

Her room was dark. The candle had burned out—or hadn't been lit.

He reached the ledge and looked through the glass. For a moment, he couldn't see anything; the night was moonless, the room a void. Then his eyes adjusted, and he made out the shape of her: not in bed, not watching for him, but curled in the chair by the window, her head tilted at an angle that would pain her when she woke.

She looked small. That was what struck him—how small she seemed, how diminished, as though the days had worn away at her edges. Her face was pale, even in the darkness; her hands were wrapped in the sleeves of her dressing gown as though she'd been cold.

He had done this. He had left her waiting, night after night, without explanation. He had been so consumed by his nephew's fever that he hadn't thought—no, that wasn't true. He had thought of her constantly. He had simply been unable to leave, unable to send word, unable to do anything but sit by a child's bed and watch him fight for breath.

He lifted his hand to tap on the glass—and stopped.

What if she didn't want him anymore? What if three nights of silence had convinced her that he was unreliable, that he had given up, that whatever they had wasn't worth the climbing? What if she had decided, sensibly, to commit herself to the life she had chosen before he had shimmied up a drainage pipe and complicated everything?

He should go. He should descend, return home, write her a letter explaining his absence. He should give her the space to decide whether she wanted to see him again.

He tapped on the glass.

She stirred. Her head lifted; she made a sound, small and confused. Then she turned toward the window, and he saw her face in the faint light from the stars—saw the tear-tracks on her cheeks, the redness around her eyes, the exhaustion written into every line.

She saw him. Her expression didn't change. For a long moment, she simply looked, as though confirming that he was real.

Then she was out of the chair, fumbling with the latch, pulling the window open.

"You didn't come."

The words came out flat, without accusation. He climbed through and stood before her, suddenly uncertain what to do with his hands.

"I couldn't. My nephew—my sister's child—he was ill. I was there for four days. I couldn't leave, and I couldn't send word, and I—"

"You didn't come." She repeated it, and her voice cracked on the second word, and then she was crying, her hands over her face, her shoulders shaking with the force of it.

He reached for her without thinking. She came into his arms as though she had been waiting for exactly this, her face pressed against his chest, her fingers curling into the back of his coat. He held her while she cried, and he felt his own eyes sting, and he didn't try to stop it.

"I'm sorry," he said. "I'm sorry. I should have found a way. I should have—"

"You didn't come." She said it a third time, and then she was laughing, or crying, or something between the two. "I thought you were dead. I thought you'd fallen. I thought you'd decided I wasn't—that this wasn't—"

"Never." The word came out fierce, more certain than anything he'd ever said. "I would never decide that. Eleanor. Ellie. Look at me."

She looked up. Her face was wet, her eyes swollen, her hair escaping its plait in tangled strands. She was, he thought, the most beautiful thing he had ever seen.

"I didn't come because I couldn't," he said. "Not because I didn't want to. Every night I thought of you. Every hour I wondered if you were waiting. If you were—"

"I waited." She wiped her face with her sleeve, a gesture so unguarded it made his chest ache. "Three nights. I lit the candle and I waited, and you didn't come, and I thought—"

"I know. I'm sorry. I'm so sorry."

They stood there, in the dark room, holding each other. The window was still open; cold air came through, but neither moved to close it.

"I should have sent word," he said. "I should have found a way. But Thomas—my nephew—he was so sick, and Catherine needed me, and I kept thinking I would leave tomorrow, tomorrow, and then tomorrow came and he was worse."

"I understand." She pulled back slightly, looked at him. "Your hand. It's infected."

He glanced down. The bandage, unchanged for four days, was stained and discolored. The skin around the wound was red and swollen.

"It's nothing."

"It's not nothing. Sit down. Let me—"

"Later." He caught her wrist, held it. "Eleanor. I have to tell you something."

She stilled.

"I have nothing to offer you." The words came hard, each one distinct. "I'm an architect. I make a decent living—a comfortable living, for a single man. But I don't have eleven thousand pounds. I don't have connections or a title or the ability to save your family from ruin. I have two rooms above a bookshop, and a sister who worries about me, and a hand that won't stop bleeding because I keep climbing drainage pipes to reach you."

She didn't speak.

"I can't solve your problems," he continued. "I can't pay your father's debts or give your sister a London season or rescue you from Mr. Creswell. All I have is myself, and that's not—I know it's not enough."

"Arthur—"

"Let me finish. I've been thinking about this for four days, sitting in my sister's kitchen, watching her child fight for breath. And I realized something. I can't offer you security. I can't offer you comfort or position or any of the things you're supposed to want. But I can offer you—"

"What?" Her voice was barely a whisper.

"This." He raised her hand to his mouth, pressed his lips to her knuckles. "Me. However imperfect. However inadequate. I can climb to your window every night for the rest of my life, if that's what you want. I can love you from a distance if that's all that's possible. But I need to know—do you want that? Do you want me? Because if you don't, if you've decided that Mr. Creswell and security and duty matter more—"

She kissed him.

He had no time to think, no time to prepare. Her mouth found his, and the world narrowed to the pressure of her lips, the warmth of her hands cupping his face, the desperate clumsiness of two people who had never done this before and couldn't bear to stop.

When they broke apart, she was breathing hard. Her hands were still on his face.

"I don't care," she said. "I don't care about security. I don't care about position. I spent three nights thinking you were dead, and I realized—I can't do this without you. I can't be who they want me to be if it means losing you."

"Eleanor—"

"I'm still going to marry him." The words fell between them, cold and final. "I don't see a way out. I've looked. I've thought about it every night, and I can't—my family—"

"I know."

"But I want you to keep coming. I want whatever we can have. I want—"

"What?"

"This." She kissed him again, slower this time, and he felt something break open inside him—something he hadn't known was sealed.

They didn't speak after that. They sat together on her narrow bed, his arm around her, her head on his shoulder, her hand resting on his chest. Outside, the sky began to lighten. Neither moved.

When the first true light of dawn came, she turned her face into his neck.

"I'm glad you came back," she said.

"I'll always come back."

"Don't make promises you can't keep."

"I'm not." He pressed his lips to her hair. "I'll always come back. Whatever happens. Whatever you decide. I'll climb to this window until my hands give out."

She laughed, a wet sound. "Your hands are already giving out."

"Then I'll find another way."

She tilted her head up. Her eyes were red-rimmed, her face blotchy, her hair a disaster. He had never loved anything so completely.

"Go," she said. "Before someone sees."

"I don't want to."

"I know. But you have to."

He kissed her one more time—brief, hard, a promise—and then he was at the window, climbing down into the morning.

She watched him go. He felt her eyes on him all the way to the ground.

# Chapter Six — The Decency of Strangers

Seven candles burned on the dining table.

Eleanor had counted them twice, arranging and rearranging them in her mind, because counting was safer than listening—to her mother's effusive praise of Mr. Creswell's family, to her father's rambling stories about the estate's former prosperity, to Flora's breathless questions about the wedding dress and whether there would be a breakfast afterward.

The candles were beeswax, expensive, brought out for occasions that warranted expense. Seven candles for seven places: her parents at either end, Mr. Creswell beside Eleanor, Flora beyond him, and Aunt Potter, who had traveled from Somerset for the occasion, completing the tableau. The roast was excellent; Cook had outdone herself. The wine was from the cellar her father had been depleting steadily for three years.

"You must see the grounds tomorrow, Mr. Creswell," her mother was saying. "The east garden, in particular, is quite lovely in autumn. The roses are past, of course, but the structure of the thing—the landscaping—my husband has done wonders with what he inherited."

Mr. Creswell nodded earnestly. "I should like that very much, Mrs. Blackwood. I understand there's a stream? I'm fond of water. The sound of it, I mean. There's a river near our house in Lancashire—not grand, you understand, but pleasant. Good for walking beside."

"Indeed, yes. The stream runs along the lower meadow. Eleanor can show you. She knows the grounds better than anyone."

Eleanor felt Mr. Creswell's glance slide toward her. She kept her eyes on her plate, on the half-eaten roast she couldn't bring herself to finish.

"Miss Blackwood?" His voice was gentle. "Would that be agreeable? A walk tomorrow morning, perhaps, before I return?"

"Of course." The words came automatically. "I should like that."

Flora bounced in her seat. "May I come? I know the best path, the one that goes past the old orchard. Eleanor used to climb the trees there when we were children, before—well, before everything."

"Flora." Their mother's voice carried a warning.

"I'm only saying—"

"Flora, that's enough."

Mr. Creswell looked between them, his expression uncertain. "I should be delighted if Miss Flora joined us. And Mrs. Blackwood, if you're feeling well enough—"

"My health doesn't permit walking, I'm afraid." Mrs. Blackwood smiled, the smile that meant she had won something. "But I'm sure Eleanor will be a capable guide. She knows every corner of this estate."

The conversation turned to the wedding—the church, the guest list, the breakfast that would follow. Eleanor listened without hearing, nodding when appropriate, smiling when required. She had become expert at this, at being present while absent, at wearing her face like a mask that could be raised or lowered at will.

The candles burned lower. The wine was replenished. Her father told a story he had told a dozen times before, about a hunting party in the days before the inheritance, when they had been people who didn't hunt but had been invited anyway. Mr. Creswell laughed in the right places. Flora yawned and was sent to bed.

And then, over the remains of the final course, Mr. Creswell turned to Eleanor and said, "I wonder if I might have a moment of your time. In private. If that's agreeable."

The room went still. Her mother's fork paused halfway to her mouth; her father's glass hovered at his lips. Even Aunt Potter, who had been nodding over her wine, looked up with sudden alertness.

"Of course," Eleanor heard herself say. "Shall we go to the drawing room?"

The drawing room was cold. No fire had been laid; the servants hadn't expected them to retire here after dinner. Eleanor lit a single lamp and turned to find Mr. Creswell standing near the door, his hands clasped before him like a man about to make a speech.

He was nervous. She could see it in the way he held his shoulders, the slight sheen on his forehead despite the chill. He touched his collar, adjusted his cuffs, cleared his throat.

"Miss Blackwood," he began. "Eleanor. May I call you Eleanor?"

She nodded.

"I asked to speak with you because—because there are things that ought to be said, and I haven't said them, and I find I cannot—" He stopped, tried again. "My whole life, I have been the third son. The unnecessary one. My brothers were the heirs; I was the spare. I was sent away to school and then to the business, and I was told to be useful, and I tried, but I always knew I was—filling space. Taking up room that wasn't really mine."

Eleanor waited. The lamplight cast strange shadows on his face, softening the features she had thought ungainly.

"When I met you," he continued, "I thought—here is someone who might understand. Someone who knows what it is to be positioned, to be used for family advantage. I thought we might—we might suit each other. Not because of passion or romance, but because we both know what it is to be without choices."

She felt something cold settle in her chest.

"I know this engagement is not—" He gestured vaguely, helplessly. "I know it is not a love match. I know you did not seek me out, did not choose me from among many suitors. I know what your family needs, and I am happy to provide it. But I want you to know that I also—I hope—"

He stepped closer. His eyes were wet; she could see the lamplight reflected in them.

"I hope you might come to care for me. In time. I know I am not—what women dream of. I am not handsome or dashing or clever in the ways that matter at parties. But I am steady. I am kind, I think, or I try to be. And I would spend my whole life trying to make you glad you married me. If you would let me."

She couldn't speak. The silence stretched between them, filling with all the things she ought to say—thank you, you're kind, I'll try—and none of them would come, because every one of them was a lie.

"I don't expect an answer," he said, misreading her silence. "I only wanted you to know. What I feel. What I hope for." He laughed, a small, embarrassed sound. "I've never said any of this to anyone. I'm not saying it well."

"You're saying it beautifully." The words came out before she could stop them, and she meant them, and that made it worse.

He smiled, a transformed look. "Then I'll leave you to your rest. Tomorrow—the walk? You'll still—"

"Yes."

"Good. Good." He moved toward the door, then paused. "I know I'm not the man you would have chosen. But I want you to know—I see you. The real you. The one who watches and waits and thinks things she doesn't say. I see her, and I admire her, and I—"

He stopped, shook his head, and was gone.

Eleanor stood in the cold drawing room, alone with the lamp and the silence and the terrible weight of her own cruelty.

---

She was weeping when Arthur climbed through the window.

He saw it immediately—her face in the candlelight, wet and contorted—and crossed to her in three strides, pulling her against him before she could speak.

"What happened? What is it?"

She couldn't answer. The sobs came from somewhere deep, somewhere she hadn't known existed, and they shook her whole body. He held her through them, one hand stroking her hair, the other pressed flat against her back.

When the worst had passed, he pulled back to look at her. "Tell me."

"He's kind." She heard her own voice as though from a distance—raw, broken. "He's kind, Arthur. He spoke to me tonight, and he said—he wants me to be happy. He sees me. He sees who I really am, and he's kind, and I'm going to destroy him."

Arthur's expression flickered—something crossed his face, there and gone.

"You're not destroying him," he said carefully. "You're choosing not to marry him. There's a difference."

"There isn't. Not really. He hopes—I saw it in his face—he hopes I'll come to care for him. And I can't. I can't because I—"

She stopped. The words she hadn't said, had barely admitted to herself, hung between them.

"Because you what?"

"Because I love you." She said it to her hands, not to his face. "Because I love you, and it doesn't matter that he's kind or steady or that he sees me. I can't give him what he wants because I've already given it to you, and I didn't even mean to, it just—happened. And now I have to marry him anyway, because my family needs—"

"No."

She looked up. Arthur's face was pale, his jaw set.

"What?"

"No. You're not going to marry him."

"Arthur—"

"I don't have a plan." He said it rapidly, the words tumbling over each other. "I don't have money or connections or any of the things you're supposed to want. I have two rooms and a sister and a hand that won't heal. But I'll take you away from this. Tonight, tomorrow, whenever you say. We'll go somewhere—they can't follow if we go far enough. I'll find work. You'll—you'll have to leave your family, I know that, I know what I'm asking, but—"

"Yes."

He stopped. "What?"

"Yes." She rose, stood before him, and took his face in her hands. "Yes. Take me away. I don't care where. I don't care if we're poor or scandalous or disowned. I can't do this anymore. I can't sit at dinner tables and smile at kind men and know I'm going to ruin them. I can't watch my mother count the silver and my father drink himself to sleep and Flora dream of London seasons I'll never be able to give her. I can't—"

Her voice cracked. He pulled her close, her face against his chest, and she felt his heart beating fast beneath her cheek.

"When?" he asked.

"The wedding contract is signed in six days. If I'm going to leave, it has to be before. Three nights from now. That gives me time to—to pack what I can carry. To write a letter. To say—"

She stopped. To say goodbye. To walk away from everything she had ever known.

"Three nights," he agreed. "I'll have a carriage ready. We'll go north—my sister has friends in Edinburgh, I can find work there. It's far enough that the scandal won't reach, or if it does, no one will care."

"Edinburgh." She tried to picture it—a city she had never seen, a life she had never imagined. "I've never been farther than Bath."

"Neither have I." He laughed, a slightly wild sound. "We'll learn together."

She looked up at him. His face was close, familiar now in ways it hadn't been a week ago.

"I'm afraid," she said.

"I know."

"I'm choosing you over my family. Over my duty. Over everything I was raised to believe mattered."

"I know."

"Is that wrong?"

He was quiet for a moment. Outside, the wind stirred the bare branches of the oak; she could hear them scratching against the sky.

"I think," he said slowly, "that there isn't a right choice. Only choices, and what you do after. You can choose them and be miserable, or choose me and be happy, and either way something is lost. But at least—with me—you won't be alone."

She kissed him then, tasting salt on her lips, feeling the desperate gratitude in his response. When they broke apart, they were both trembling.

"Three nights," she said.

"Three nights."

They held each other as the candle burned down, planning nothing, promising everything, watching the window for a dawn that would bring them closer to leaving.
